h1Title: Follicular Lymphoma Grade I, Intra-Abdominal Lymph Nodes: Symptoms & Treatment
heroOverview: Follicular lymphoma is the second most common non-Hodgkin lymphoma after diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, accounting for approximately 20% of all lymphomas. It is a B-cell neoplasm composed of germinal centre B cells with a characteristic follicular growth pattern and the t(14;18) translocation present in 85-90% of cases, leading to BCL2 overexpression and resistance to apoptosis. Intra-abdominal lymph node involvement is extremely common at presentation, with the abdomen being the most frequently involved nodal site. The disease typically follows an indolent but largely incurable course with standard therapy. Stage III-IV disease is present at diagnosis in approximately 80% of patients. While the median survival exceeds 15 years with modern therapy, the disease follows a pattern of repeated relapses with decreasing treatment-free intervals. Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab combined with chemotherapy is the standard of care for advanced-stage or symptomatic disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2-4 per 100,000 annually in Western countries; represents 20% of all non-Hodgkin lymphomas and 35% of all indolent lymphomas
keyStats.demographics: Slight female predominance (F:M 1.2-1.5:1); median age at diagnosis 60-65 years; rare under age 30
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 60-65 years; incidence rises progressively with age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100,000-150,000 new cases per year worldwide; higher in Western countries

definition: Follicular lymphoma Grade I (ICD-10: C83.1, Grade 1 with /2 behavior if unresolved; here treated with intra-abdominal nodal involvement) is an indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma arising from germinal centre B cells with a characteristic follicular growth pattern. The WHO classification grades follicular lymphoma on centrocyte density: Grade 1 has 0-5 centroblasts per follicle (centrocyte:centroblast ratio high), Grade 2 has 6-15 centroblasts per follicle, and Grade 3 has more than 15 centroblasts per follicle with further subdivision into 3a (centrocytes present) and 3b (pure centroblasts). Grade 1-2 follicular lymphoma is considered low-grade. The defining molecular lesion is the t(14;18)(q32;q21) translocation, which places BCL2 under the control of the immunoglobulin heavy chain enhancer, causing constitutive BCL2 overexpression and inhibition of apoptosis. Disease commonly presents with painless generalized lymphadenopathy, and intra-abdominal nodes are among the most frequently affected sites. The disease has a characteristic pattern of sequential relapses with progressively shorter treatment-free intervals. Transformation to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (histological transformation) occurs in approximately 3-5% per year and carries a poor prognosis.

typesClassification.type: Follicular lymphoma Grade 1 (0-5 centroblasts per high-power field)
typesClassification.description: The lowest grade with the most favourable prognosis; most indolent behaviour; may have very slow progression over years.

typesClassification.type: Follicular lymphoma Grade 2 (6-15 centroblasts per high-power field)
typesClassification.description: Intermediate grade; clinically similar to Grade 1 but with slightly higher proliferative index and marginally worse prognosis.

typesClassification.type: Follicular lymphoma Grade 3a (centrocytes present, >15 centroblasts per HPF)
typesClassification.description: Grade 3 with centrocytes present; behaves more like follicular lymphoma than DLBCL; treated with similar approach to Grade 1-2.
Pure centroblastic; more aggressive and often treated similarly to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
A distinct variant presenting as small intestinal polyps, typically in the duodenum; has a different molecular profile (IRF4 translocation) and excellent prognosis.

typesClassification.type: Primary nodal versus primary extranodal follicular lymphoma
typesClassification.description: Most FL presents in lymph nodes; intra-abdominal nodes are common. Primary extranodal presentation (e.g., skin, spleen) has distinct clinical features.

primarySymptoms: Painless, gradually enlarging lymph nodes in the abdomen detected incidentally on CT or ultrasound performed for unrelated indications.
Abdominal discomfort or fullness from bulky intra-abdominal lymphadenopathy compressing the stomach, bowel, or ureters.
Constitutional B-symptoms — unexplained fever above 38°C, drenching night sweats, or unintentional weight loss exceeding 10% of body weight.
Early satiety and nausea from gastric compression by enlarged celiac or para-aortic lymph nodes.
Lower limb oedema from iliac or inguinal nodal compression of lymphatic drainage.
Palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy (cervical, axillary, or inguinal) accompanying intra-abdominal disease in over 50% of cases.
Fatigue, exercise intolerance, and dyspnoea from anaemia secondary to bone marrow involvement or hypersplenism.
Pruritus after a hot shower or bath from histamine release by the lymphoma cells.
Abdominal distension and visible mass in advanced intra-abdominal disease.
Pleural effusion or ascites from bulky mediastinal or peritoneal disease involvement.
earlyWarningSigns: Painless progressive lymph node enlargement over weeks to months
Unexplained B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) in an adult over 50
New abdominal discomfort, early satiety, or distension without gastrointestinal explanation
Lower limb oedema suggesting pelvic or inguinal lymph node obstruction
Persistent fatigue with anaemia not explained by other causes
Pruritus after hot shower or bath
emergencySigns: Rapidly enlarging abdominal mass with pain suggesting transformation to aggressive lymphoma — requires urgent biopsy
Ureteric obstruction with hydronephrosis and renal impairment from pelvic or iliac lymphadenopathy
Bowel obstruction or ischaemia from mesenteric nodal compression
Spinal cord compression from vertebral body or paraspinal lymph node involvement
Severe anaemia with haemodynamic instability from bone marrow failure or hypersplenism

causes.description: Present in 85-90% of follicular lymphoma cases. The translocation places BCL2 (an anti-apoptotic protein) under the control of the immunoglobulin heavy chain enhancer, causing BCL2 overexpression. This blocks the intrinsic apoptotic pathway and allows germinal centre B cells to escape apoptosis, accumulating genetic mutations.

causes.cause: Germinal centre B-cell origin with accumulated mutations
causes.description: FL cells derive from germinal centre centrocytes that have undergone iterative rounds of somatic hypermutation in the dark zone. This process generates the BCL2 translocation and accumulates secondary mutations in EZH2, CREBBP, MEF2B, and TNFRSF14 that drive clonal evolution and transformation.

FL incidence rises sharply after age 50, suggesting immune senescence and loss of immune surveillance contribute to lymphomagenesis. Impaired DNA repair, chronic antigen stimulation, and reduced cytotoxic T-cell function create an environment permissive for B-cell transformation.

Pesticide and herbicide exposure (particularly phenoxyacetic acid herbicides) is consistently associated with elevated FL risk in agricultural workers. Organic solvents and hair dye use have been inconsistently implicated. EBV association is weak in FL compared to other NHL subtypes.

causes.cause: Prior immunosuppressive therapy or autoimmune disease
causes.description: Patients with Sjögren syndrome or rheumatoid arthritis have a 15-40-fold elevated risk of FL, mediated by chronic lymphoid stimulation. Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD) can mimic FL; HIV infection is associated with a 5-8-fold increased risk.

riskFactors.description: Median age at diagnosis is 60-65 years; FL is uncommon under 30 and typically more aggressive when it occurs in young adults.
First-degree relatives of FL patients have a 2-3-fold elevated risk of FL and other lymphoproliferative disorders.

riskFactors.factor: Prior autoimmune disease (Sjögren syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis)
riskFactors.description: Chronic immune stimulation and dysregulation from autoimmune disease significantly elevates FL risk.
Agricultural workers with prolonged phenoxyherbicide exposure have elevated FL risk; organic farming reduces exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Prior solid organ transplantation and immunosuppression
riskFactors.description: Chronic immunosuppression post-transplant allows Epstein-Barr virus-driven lymphoproliferation resembling FL.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex is associated with slightly higher risk, while female predominance is seen in some series
riskFactors.description: Sex ratio varies; female predominance reported in some populations, possibly related to hormonal factors.

affectedDemographics: Most common in adults aged 55-65; rare under age 30 (less than 5% of cases).
Slight female predominance (F:M 1.2-1.5:1) in most Western population studies.
Higher incidence in North America and Europe (2-4 per 100,000) compared to Asia and Africa (0.5-1 per 100,000), partly due to genetic and environmental factors.
Incidence is rising by approximately 1-2% per year for reasons that remain unclear but may relate to improved diagnostics and changing environmental exposures.
Slightly higher incidence in higher socioeconomic groups.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires excisional lymph node biopsy for adequate tissue to assess architecture, grading, and immunophenotype. Fine needle aspiration is insufficient for initial diagnosis due to inability to assess follicular architecture. Core biopsy may suffice if the node is not easily accessible but carries a risk of sampling error. The biopsy should be reviewed by an experienced haematopathologist. Characteristic findings include follicular architecture with crowding and back-to-back follicles, centrocytes (small cleaved cells with irregular nuclear contours), and absence of polarization. Immunophenotype by flow cytometry shows CD19+, CD20+, CD10+, BCL6+, BCL2+, and IRF4/MUM1 negative (germinal centre phenotype). BCL2 staining by IHC is positive in 85-90% reflecting the t(14;18). Ki-67 index is typically low in Grade 1-2. Bone marrow biopsy is mandatory for staging. CT neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis defines nodal disease extent and bulky sites. PET-CT is increasingly used and helps identify metabolically active sites for biopsy in suspected transformation. Laboratory work-up includes FBC, LDH, beta-2-microglobulin, uric acid, liver function, hepatitis B/C, and HIV serology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosis; provides adequate tissue for architecture, grading, and IHC
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case surgical biopsy under local or general anesthesia; 5-10mm node excised; results in 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory for staging; detects marrow involvement in 40-50% of cases and informs prognosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Iliac crest biopsy under local anesthesia; results in 5-7 days; combined with imaging for FLIPI calculation.

diagnosticTests.test: CT neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Standard anatomical staging; identifies nodal sites, bulky disease (>7cm), and organ involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-minute scan; IV and oral contrast; results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metabolically active disease sites; identifies occult disease and guides biopsy in suspected transformation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: FDG injection, 60-minute uptake, whole-body scan; results in 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Flow cytometry of lymph node or bone marrow
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms B-cell immunophenotype (CD19, CD20, CD10, BCL2), kappa/lambda restriction, and clonality
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh tissue submitted in transport medium; 2-3 days for results.

diagnosticTests.test: BCL2 IHC and FISH for t(14;18)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms BCL2 overexpression and t(14;18) translocation; supports diagnosis and distinguishes from reactive follicular hyperplasia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on biopsy tissue; 3-5 days alongside routine pathology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Baseline laboratory markers; LDH and beta-2-microglobulin are prognostic FLIPI variables; cytopenias indicate bone marrow involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests; results within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Essential before rituximab-based therapy; HBV reactivation can be fatal with immunosuppression; HIV affects treatment choice and prognosis

treatmentOverview: Management of follicular lymphoma is stratified by stage, disease burden, symptoms, and patient fitness. Stage I-II limited disease is treated with involved-site radiation therapy (ISRT) 24-30 Gy in 12-15 fractions with curative potential; approximately 40-50% of patients are cured with radiation alone. Stage III-IV or advanced disease has two paradigms: watchful waiting for asymptomatic low-burden disease (FLIPI low-risk, no B-symptoms, no cytopenias, no bulky disease), or chemoimmunotherapy for symptomatic or high-burden disease. For advanced symptomatic disease, first-line options include R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone), R-CVP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, vincristine, prednisolone), or bendamustine plus rituximab (BR). Obinutuzumab (anti-CD20, glycoengineered) with chemotherapy is an alternative. Maintenance rituximab every 2 months for 2 years significantly improves PFS. For relapse, options depend on prior treatment and duration of response: re-challenge with same regimen if remission >12-24 months; alternative chemotherapy plus anti-CD20; PI3K inhibitors (idelalisib, copanlisib) for relapsed disease; EZH2 inhibitor tazemetostat for EZH2-mutant FL; or CAR-T cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel) for transform or triple-class-exposed relapsed FL. Autologous or allogeneic stem cell transplant is considered in eligible patients with chemosensitive relapse.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab + bendamustine (BR)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line chemoimmunotherapy for advanced-stage symptomatic follicular lymphoma; bendamustine 90mg/m2 days 1-2 with rituximab 375mg/m2 day 1, every 28 days for 6 cycles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 90-95%; CR 40-50%; median PFS 60+ months; superior to R-CHOP for FL in terms of toxicity profile and possibly efficacy

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab + CHOP (R-CHOP)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard chemoimmunotherapy for higher-grade or transformed FL; cyclophosphamide 750mg/m2, doxorubicin 50mg/m2, vincristine 1.4mg/m2 day 1, prednisolone 100mg days 1-5.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 90-95%; CR 50-60%; 5-year PFS 60-70%; cardiac toxicity limits use in patients with cardiac comorbidity

medicalTreatments.name: Obinutuzumab + chemotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Glycoengineered anti-CD20 with enhanced ADCC; combined with bendamustine or CHOP for first-line advanced FL (GALLIUM trial).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improved PFS over rituximab-based regimens (3-year PFS 80% vs 71%); higher rates of infusion reactions and neutropenia

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab maintenance every 2 months for 2 years
medicalTreatments.description: Standard following induction chemoimmunotherapy for advanced FL; improves progression-free and overall survival.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces risk of progression by 50%; improves OS at 3-5 years; continued beyond 2 years is under study

medicalTreatments.name: Tazemetostat 800mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: EZH2 inhibitor approved for relapsed/refractory FL with EZH2 mutation (20-25% of cases); oral targeted therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 35% in EZH2-mutant FL; 50% in EZH2 wild-type; median DoR 13 months; well-tolerated with anaemia and thrombocytopenia as main toxicities

medicalTreatments.name: Idelalisib 150mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: PI3K delta inhibitor approved for relapsed FL after two prior systemic therapies; targets B-cell receptor signaling.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 55-60%; median PFS 11 months; significant toxicity including transaminitis, diarrhoea, and infections requiring monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: Lenalidomide + rituximab (R-squared)
medicalTreatments.description: Immunomodulatory agent with rituximab for relapsed FL; oral regimen with manageable toxicity profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 76%; median PFS 39 months; an alternative for chemo-refractory disease

medicalTreatments.name: Axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) CAR-T
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy; approved for relapsed FL after two prior lines of therapy including anthracycline and anti-CD20.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 94%; CR 80%; 18-month PFS 57%; high rates of cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurotoxicity (ICANS)

surgicalOptions.name: Excisional lymph node biopsy for diagnosis
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive diagnostic procedure; provides adequate tissue for histopathology, grading, and immunophenotyping.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic in >95% of cases when performed by an experienced surgeon and reviewed by a specialist haematopathologist

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic staging of intra-abdominal nodes
surgicalOptions.description: Diagnostic laparoscopy with nodal biopsy for suspected intra-abdominal FL without accessible peripheral nodes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Provides tissue diagnosis and staging for intra-abdominal disease with low morbidity

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive surgery for bulky abdominal disease
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely required; considered for symptomatic relief in patients with ureteric obstruction, bowel obstruction, or refractory pain from massive intra-abdominal lymphadenopathy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief in 60-70%; often followed by systemic therapy

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting (active surveillance)
alternativeTreatments.description: Appropriate for asymptomatic low-burden advanced FL with low FLIPI score; treatment deferred until symptoms develop or disease progresses. Approximately 20-30% of newly diagnosed FL patients are managed this way at presentation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Single-agent rituximab
alternativeTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monotherapy for patients who prefer to avoid chemotherapy or are unfit for combination therapy; less effective than chemoimmunotherapy.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 60-70%; CR 10-20%; slower onset of response compared to combination therapy

alternativeTreatments.name: Radioimmunotherapy (Y-90 ibritumomab tiuxetan)
alternativeTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 antibody linked to Yttrium-90; delivers targeted radiation to CD20+ cells; considered for consolidation or relapsed disease.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: ORR 70-80%; CR 30-40%; useful in patients with comorbidities precluding chemotherapy

whySeeSpecialist: Follicular lymphoma management is complex and requires a haematologist-oncologist with lymphoma expertise. Watch-and-wait versus treatment decisions, choice of chemoimmunotherapy regimen, duration of maintenance, and management of relapsed disease all require specialist input. Referral to a lymphoma centre is recommended.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a haematology centre with a dedicated lymphoma service, weekly multidisciplinary team meeting, and access to CAR-T, clinical trials, and transplant. Ask about their FL volume, approach to watch-and-wait, and experience with novel agents like tazemetostat and PI3K inhibitors.
hospitalCriteria: Dedicated lymphoma service with weekly MDT
Clinical trials programme with FL portfolio
Oncology pharmacy with novel agent access
keyFacilities: Lymphoma MDT with haematologist, radiation oncologist, pathologist, and specialist nurse
Stem cell transplant unit (autologous and allogeneic)
preventionStrategies: No specific preventive measures for sporadic FL.
Occupational safety measures to reduce pesticide and herbicide exposure in agricultural workers.
Early recognition and treatment of autoimmune disease to reduce chronic immune stimulation.
No screening is recommended for the general population as FL is rare and no validated screening tool exists.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid smoking; tobacco is associated with a modestly elevated FL risk.
Maintain healthy weight with balanced diet and regular exercise; obesity is associated with elevated FL risk.
Engage in moderate regular physical activity; improves quality of life during and after treatment.
Avoid raw or undercooked foods during chemotherapy-induced neutropenia.
Seek psychological support for anxiety related to living with an incurable but often manageable lymphoma.

recommended: Balanced diet rich in fruit, vegetables, whole grains, and lean protein to support immune function during treatment
High-protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to prevent muscle wasting during chemotherapy
Small frequent meals if early satiety from bulky abdominal nodes
Iron-rich foods if anaemic from bone marrow involvement
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat, fish, and eggs during neutropenic periods
Excessive alcohol (hepatotoxicity risk with chemotherapy, interferes with immune recovery)
High-sodium processed foods to manage fluid retention from supportive medications
Grapefruit juice with idelalisib or tazemetostat (CYP3A4 interactions)

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week) is encouraged throughout treatment and recovery. It improves fatigue, mood, and cardiovascular health. Avoid strenuous exercise during neutropenic periods. Resume gradually after intensive chemotherapy.
dailyManagement: Take prophylactic medications (antivirals for HBV, PCP prophylaxis with cotrimoxazole) as prescribed during and after treatment.
Report new lymph node enlargement, B-symptoms, unexplained weight loss, or fever to the haematology team promptly.
Attend scheduled surveillance CT or PET scans; early detection of relapse enables treatment before symptoms develop.
Monitor temperature at home during chemotherapy and report fever above 38°C for neutropenic fever assessment.
Keep a record of prior treatment regimens to inform future therapy decisions.
prognosis: Follicular lymphoma is indolent but largely incurable with standard therapy. The FLIPI (Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index) stratifies patients into three risk groups: low (0-1 risk factors, median OS not reached at 10 years), intermediate (2 risk factors, median OS 5-10 years), and poor (3-5 risk factors, median OS 3-5 years). Risk factors are: age >60, Ann Arbor stage III-IV, haemoglobin <12 g/dL, elevated LDH, >4 nodal sites. Modern therapy with anti-CD20 chemoimmunotherapy has improved median OS to 15-20 years. Transformation to DLBCL occurs at a rate of approximately 3% per year and carries a median OS of 2-4 years despite aggressive treatment. Patients who achieve complete metabolic remission on PET-CT after induction have the best long-term outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: After chemotherapy (BR or R-CHOP), neutropenia typically occurs around days 10-14 and recovers by day 21-28. Full blood count recovery takes 4-6 weeks. Rituximab maintenance requires 4-weekly or 2-monthly visits for 2 years. Relapse may occur years to decades after initial treatment; long-term follow-up is lifelong.
complications: Transformation to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) with aggressive behaviour, rapid nodal growth, and high LDH — requires biopsy confirmation and treatment as high-grade NHL.
Bone marrow failure with neutropenia, anaemia, and thrombocytopenia — requires growth factors, transfusion, and infection prophylaxis.
Ureteric obstruction with hydronephrosis from pelvic lymphadenopathy — requires ureteric stenting or percutaneous nephrostomy.
Bowel obstruction from mesenteric nodal compression — may require surgical bypass or stenting.
Hypersplenism with pancytopenia from splenic involvement — managed with splenic irradiation or splenectomy in refractory cases.
Secondary malignancies including MDS/AML (from alkylating agent chemotherapy) and solid tumours — long-term surveillance required.
Immune-mediated cytopenias including autoimmune haemolytic anaemia and ITP — managed with steroids, rituximab, or splenectomy.
Progressive disease with refractory cytopenias and eventual treatment failure — consider clinical trial, CAR-T, or palliative care.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Action (UK) — follicular lymphoma information
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing detailed information on FL, treatment options, clinical trials, and support services.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Research Foundation (US) — follicular lymphoma resources
supportResources.description: US patient organisation with FL-specific resources, clinical trial matching, and patient education programmes.

supportResources.name: Follicular Lymphoma Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient-founded charitable foundation focused exclusively on FL, providing information, community, and research funding.

supportResources.name: Bloodwise / Blood Cancer UK — non-Hodgkin lymphoma
supportResources.description: UK charity with extensive information on FL, patient stories, and support for people affected by blood cancer.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: DLBCL is an aggressive lymphoma presenting with rapidly enlarging masses, B-symptoms, and high LDH. FL is indolent with slowly progressive lymphadenopathy and low Ki-67. Transformation from FL to DLBCL can occur; new rapid growth in FL warrants urgent biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Marginal Zone Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MZL is a distinct indolent B-cell lymphoma arising from marginal zone B cells. It lacks the t(14;18) BCL2 translocation and follicular growth pattern of FL. Immunophenotype (CD10 negative, BCL2 positive) and morphology distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia (CLL)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CLL presents primarily with peripheral blood lymphocytosis and bone marrow involvement. FL presents with nodal disease and rarely has significant peripheral blood involvement. Flow cytometry (CLL: CD5+CD23+; FL: CD10+CD23-) distinguishes them.

relatedConditions.name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: FL is a subtype of indolent B-cell NHL. Treatment principles and classification share common frameworks with other NHL subtypes.

relatedConditions.name: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: DLBCL is the most common aggressive NHL and the most frequent histology into which FL transforms. Management of transformed FL follows DLBCL protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Marginal Zone Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another indolent B-cell NHL that shares clinical features with FL but has distinct biology and management considerations.

faqs.question: What is follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Follicular lymphoma (FL) is a slow-growing (indolent) B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma arising from germinal centre B cells. It is the second most common NHL, accounting for approximately 20% of cases. Most cases present with painless lymphadenopathy and have the t(14;18) BCL2 translocation.

faqs.question: Is follicular lymphoma cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. FL is a cancer — a malignancy of lymphocytes (white blood cells). While it is slow-growing and often responds well to treatment, it is considered largely incurable with standard therapy and tends to relapse over many years.

faqs.question: What does Grade 1 follicular lymphoma mean?
faqs.answer: Grade 1 FL is the lowest grade, with 0-5 centroblasts (large cells) per follicle under microscopy. It is the most indolent form. Grades are based on the density of large cells; Grade 1-2 are considered low-grade and behave similarly.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of follicular lymphoma in intra-abdominal nodes?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include abdominal discomfort, early satiety, nausea from gastric compression by enlarged nodes, lower limb oedema from pelvic nodal obstruction, and B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) if advanced. Many patients have no symptoms.

faqs.question: How is follicular lymphoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Excisional lymph node biopsy provides the diagnosis and grading. Bone marrow biopsy is mandatory for staging. CT and PET-CT define the disease extent. The t(14;18) BCL2 translocation is present in 85-90% of cases.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Modern therapy has improved median survival to 15-20 years. The FLIPI index stratifies prognosis: low-risk patients have near-normal life expectancy. Poorer prognostic factors include high FLIPI score, transformation to DLBCL, and early relapse after treatment.

faqs.question: Can follicular lymphoma be cured?
faqs.answer: FL is largely considered incurable with standard chemoimmunotherapy because of its tendency to relapse. Stage I-II disease treated with radiation alone may be cured in 40-50% of cases. Relapse occurs over many years even in responders.

faqs.question: What is watch-and-wait for follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Watchful waiting is a standard approach for asymptomatic low-burden FL where treatment is deferred until symptoms develop. Studies show no survival disadvantage compared to early treatment, and avoids chemotherapy side effects. Approximately 20-30% of newly diagnosed patients start on watch-and-wait.

faqs.question: How is advanced follicular lymphoma treated?
faqs.answer: First-line chemoimmunotherapy for symptomatic advanced FL typically consists of bendamustine plus rituximab (BR), R-CHOP, or obinutuzumab plus chemotherapy. Rituximab maintenance for 2 years follows induction. Novel agents (tazemetostat, PI3K inhibitors, CAR-T) are used in relapse.

faqs.question: What is the t(14;18) translocation in follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: The t(14;18)(q32;q21) translocation places the BCL2 anti-apoptotic gene under the control of the immunoglobulin heavy chain enhancer. BCL2 overexpression blocks apoptosis, allowing germinal centre B cells to survive and accumulate mutations. It is present in 85-90% of FL cases.

faqs.question: What is FLIPI?
faqs.answer: The Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index (FLIPI) stratifies patients into risk groups using five factors: age >60, Ann Arbor stage III-IV, haemoglobin <12 g/dL, elevated LDH, and >4 nodal sites. Low (0-1 factors), intermediate (2 factors), and poor (3-5 factors) risk groups have different median survival.

faqs.question: Can follicular lymphoma transform?
faqs.answer: Yes. FL transforms to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) at a rate of approximately 3% per year. Transformation is suspected with new rapid nodal growth, rising LDH, B-symptoms, and new hypermetabolic lesions on PET. Biopsy is mandatory to confirm transformation.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Median overall survival for FL is 15-20 years with modern therapy. Many patients live decades with the disease managed as a chronic condition. Those with early transformation, high FLIPI score, or refractory disease have shorter survival.

faqs.question: Is there a role for stem cell transplant in follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Autologous stem cell transplant can be considered for eligible patients with chemosensitive relapse who have had multiple prior lines of therapy. Allogeneic transplant offers graft-versus-lymphoma effect but carries higher non-relapse mortality. Neither is standard first-line therapy.

faqs.question: Is CAR-T therapy available for follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) and tisagenlecleucel are approved CAR-T therapies for FL after two or more prior lines of therapy. They offer high response rates in patients who have relapsed after chemoimmunotherapy and maintenance.

faqs.question: Does diet affect follicular lymphoma outcomes?
faqs.answer: No specific diet cures or prevents FL. A balanced diet supports overall health and treatment tolerability. Avoid grapefruit with tazemetostat or idelalisib. Limit alcohol during active treatment. Maintain healthy weight as obesity is associated with elevated FL risk.

faqs.question: Should family members be screened for follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Routine family screening is not recommended as FL is not clearly heritable. First-degree relatives have a modestly elevated risk (2-3 fold) but population screening is not justified. They should report any persistent lymphadenopathy to their doctor.

faqs.question: How often does follicular lymphoma recur?
faqs.answer: FL commonly recurs after initial treatment; progression-free survival after first-line chemoimmunotherapy is typically 3-7 years. With successive relapses, remission durations shorten. Repeated treatment over many years is the norm.

faqs.question: Can you live a normal life with follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many patients with FL live normal lives with appropriate management. The disease often follows a chronic relapsing course requiring periodic treatment but allowing long periods of good health between relapses. Quality of life is generally good.

faqs.question: What is the difference between follicular and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma?
faqs.answer: FL is indolent (slow-growing) with follicular architecture and BCL2 translocation; DLBCL is aggressive (fast-growing) with diffuse architecture and no BCL2 translocation. Treatment differs: FL is managed with watchful waiting and bendamustine-rituximab; DLBCL requires urgent R-CHOP.

faqs.question: What investigations are needed before starting treatment for FL?
faqs.answer: Before treatment: full blood count, LDH, beta-2-microglobulin, hepatitis B/C, HIV, bone marrow biopsy, CT staging, and PET-CT. These establish the diagnosis, stage, and FLIPI score and identify contraindications to specific therapies.

simpleName: Follicular lymphoma grade I, intra-abdominal lymph nodes
metaTitle: Follicular Lymphoma Grade 1 (Intra-Abdominal): Symptoms, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
